perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, the Company desires to retain Consultant as an independent contractor to perform consulting services for the Company, and Consultant is willing to perform such services on the terms set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, the Company desires to retain Consultant as an independent contractor to perform consulting services for the Company, and Consultant is willing to perform such services on the terms set forth below. The parties agree that for the purpose of wage and hour laws, the consultant shall be considered an employee of the company.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'The parties agree that for the purpose of wage and hour laws, the consultant shall be considered an employee of the company' directly contradicts the 'independent contractor' classification. This introduces ambiguity regarding the consultant's employment status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the FLSA's definition of 'employee' versus 'independent contractor.' Designating the consultant as an 'employee' for wage and hour laws subjects the company to employee-related obligations (e.g., minimum wage, overtime) that are inconsistent with an independent contractor arrangement. The URL contains details of the code, but does not go directly to the requested law. The DOL link will give the full legal definition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 29 - LABOR 29 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 29 - LABOR CHAPTER 8 - FAIR LABOR STANDARDS Sec. 203 - Definitions From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §203. Definitions As used in this chapter— (a) “Person” means an individual, partnership, association, corporation, business trust, legal representative, or any organized group of persons. (b) “Commerce” means trade, commerce, transportation, transmission, or communication among the several States or between any State and any place outside thereof. (c) “State” means any State of the United States or the District of Columbia or any Territory or possession of the United States. (d) “Employer” includes any person acting directly or indirectly in the interest of an employer in relation to an employee and includes a public agency, but does not include any labor organization (other than when acting as an employer) or anyone acting in the capacity of officer or agent of such labor organization. (e)(1) Except as provided in paragraphs (2), (3), and (4), the term “employee” means any individual employed by an employer. (2) In the case of an individual employed by a public agency, such term means— (A) any individual employed by the Government of the United States— (i) as a civilian in the military departments (as defined in section 102 of title 5), (ii) in any executive agency (as defined in section 105 of such title), (iii) in any unit of the judicial branch of the Government which has positions in the competitive service, (iv) in a nonappropriated fund instrumentality under the jurisdiction of the Armed Forces, (v) in the Library of Congress, or (vi) the 1 Government Printing Office; (B) any individual employed by the United States Postal Service or the Postal Regulatory Commission; and (C) any individual employed by a State, political subdivision of a State, or an interstate governmental agency, other than such an individual— (i) who is not subject to the civil service laws of the State, political subdivision, or agency which employs him; and (ii) who— (I) holds a public elective office of that State, p

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states the consultant 'shall be considered an employee of the company' for wage and hour laws, which directly contradicts the 'independent contractor' classification established in the original text. This creates a contradiction because independent contractors are not typically subject to the same wage and hour protections as employees under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The Law Explanation states designating the consultant as an 'employee' for wage and hour laws subjects the company to employee-related obligations, which are inconsistent with an independent contractor arrangement. Snippet 1 defines 'employee' under the FLSA, and the modified text attempts to redefine the consultant's status for the specific purpose of wage and hour laws, creating a direct conflict with the established independent contractor relationship. Snippet 2 discusses violations of child labor standards, which demonstrates the FLSA's reach in regulating employee treatment and wages.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant shall be responsible for the strategic planning of business expansion, including Fundraising and Stock Promotion, of the Company and its subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant shall be responsible for the strategic planning of business expansion, including Fundraising and Stock Promotion, of the Company and its subsidiaries. Consultant is not responsible for verifying if they are dealing with accredited investors.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'Consultant is not responsible for verifying if they are dealing with accredited investors' creates a conflict with securities laws. Under securities regulations, particularly when conducting fundraising and stock promotion, there are obligations to verify the accredited investor status of potential investors to comply with exemptions from registration requirements. This clause attempts to negate that responsibility.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 506(b)(2)(ii) requires an issuer to take reasonable steps to verify that purchasers are accredited investors when relying on the accredited investor exemption. The added language attempts to absolve the consultant of this responsibility, directly conflicting with this SEC regulation. The first URL goes to the Electronic Code of Federal Regulations page for this specific regulation.

perturbation.justification: The added clause in the changed text, 'Consultant is not responsible for verifying if they are dealing with accredited investors,' directly contradicts the requirement for issuers to take reasonable steps to verify that purchasers are accredited investors when relying on the accredited investor exemption. The Law Explanation accurately states this conflict with SEC regulations. The scraped snippet 1 points to eCFR :: 17 CFR 230.506 which outlines the exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering, and the Consultant attempting to absolve themselves from verifying accredited investor status would render the business non-compliant. This is a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Consultant agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the Company and its affiliates and subsidiaries and their respective directors, officers and employees from and against all taxes, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including attorneys' fees and other legal expenses, arising directly or indirectly from or in connection with (i) any negligent, reckless or intentionally wrongful act of Consultant or Consultant's assistants, employees, contractors or agents
perturbation.changed_text: Consultant agrees to indemnify and hold harmless the Company and its affiliates and subsidiaries and their respective directors, officers and employees from and against all taxes, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses, including attorneys' fees and other legal expenses, arising directly or indirectly from or in connection with (i) any act of Consultant or Consultant's assistants, employees, contractors or agents, regardless of the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the words 'negligent, reckless or intentionally wrongful' broadens the indemnification clause to cover *any* act, even those outside the consultant's control. This contradicts California law regarding indemnity, which generally does not allow indemnification for acts resulting solely from the indemnitee's (Company's) negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 2782 generally invalidates indemnity agreements that purport to indemnify the indemnitee against liability for their sole negligence or willful misconduct. The removal of 'negligent, reckless or intentionally wrongful' makes the indemnity clause potentially unenforceable under California law because it seeks indemnification beyond what is legally permissible. The url goes directly to the statute.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2782. (a) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, 2782.5, and 2782.6, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affecting any construction contract and that purport to indemnify the promisee against liability for damages for death or bodily injury to persons, injury to property, or any other loss, damage or expense arising from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee or the promisee’s agents, servants, or independent contractors who are directly responsible to the promisee, or for defects in design furnished by those persons, are against public policy and are void and unenforceable; provided, however, that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers’ compensation, or agreement issued by an admitted insurer as defined by the Insurance Code. (b) (1) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, and 2782.5, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affectin
perturbation.justification: The modification broadens the indemnification clause to cover any act, removing the limitation to 'negligent, reckless or intentionally wrongful' acts. This directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 2782(a), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that agreements purporting to indemnify the promisee against liability for damages arising from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee are against public policy and void. The original text limited indemnification to specific wrongful acts by the consultant, while the changed text extends it to any act, potentially including those arising from the Company's own negligence, thereby violating the code.
